topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, smelling of aged paper and forgotten things. I was supposed to be looking for old photo albums, but my fingers brushed against a cardboard box tucked beneath a stack of moth-eaten blankets. Inside, nestled amongst yellowed tissue paper, was a neatly organized binder. My breath hitched. It was my mother’s binder. And within it, every single school assignment from elementary school through high school. The sheer volume of completed work, neatly cataloged and preserved, hit me like a physical blow. I sank onto an old trunk, my hands trembling as I stared at the pristine collection of homework. "What in the world is this?" I whispered, the sound thin in the silence of the attic. A hot flush crept up my neck. This wasn't just keeping things; this was an obsessive archiving of my entire academic history. I felt a sharp, sudden resentment bubble up, making my jaw clench.

[story 2]

I found the box behind the spare linen closet, and the sight of it made my stomach churn. It was a binder, heavy with the weight of years, filled spine-to-spine with every worksheet, essay, and test my mother had ever handed in. My initial reaction was a stunned silence, a complete freeze in my limbs. Then, a slow, burning anger began to build behind my eyes. I lifted a particularly childish drawing, the crayon strokes faded but still visible. "Why?" I managed to ask, my voice tight, strained with disbelief. The realization dawned—she hadn't just saved them; she had curated them. I slammed the binder shut, the sound echoing loudly in the hallway. My fists clenched so tightly my nails dug into my palms. I needed an explanation, and the lack of one was suffocating.

[story 3]

It was tucked inside an old gardening book, a secret compartment I hadn't known existed. When I pulled it out, the contents were staggering: a chronological collection of every graded paper, every project, every piece of paper my mother had ever completed for me. My eyes scanned the pages rapidly, a frantic energy seizing my chest. A wave of intense frustration washed over me, making my vision swim slightly. I felt a distinct tightening in my chest, a hot pressure behind my ribs. I flipped through the pages, noticing the meticulous handwriting, the careful corrections. "This is… excessive," I muttered, the words tasting bitter. It wasn't just preservation; it felt like control. I dropped the binder onto the floor, the impact jarring. I stood up, pacing the small room, my movements jerky and agitated. I needed to confront her about this bewildering display of meticulousness.
